text: You know, seeing your favorite franchise
fall into the hands of Disney is a bit
like seeing the corpse of your beloved
Gran fall into the hands of Ed Gynne.
You know they're going to do something
horrible to it. It's just a matter of
time. Case in point, Predator: Killer of
Killers, an animated movie from the mind
of Dan Tractenberg, who's rapidly
becoming to the Predator franchise what
Dave Fona is to Star Wars.
Anyway, the best way I can describe
Killer of Killers is that it takes the
old wouldn't it be cool to see a
Predator Fighter insert interesting
historical warrior here idea and makes
that the basis for its entire plot. The
result is a kind of anthology movie
based around a trio of short stories
with a big finale event to tie them all
together. It's a neat idea in theory,
but it's also a good example of the
concept being better than the execution.
Two of the short stories are actually
pretty decent, showcasing interesting
characters in novel settings. The third
is a bit goofy and over the top, and the
finale kind of makes me wish the whole
movie hadn't even happened. But to
explain it better, I'm going to have to
delve into each of the stories, so allow
me to illuminate you. The movie kicks
off with a group of Viking warriors on a
revenge mission against a rival tribe,
naturally led by a female warlord named
Ursa, because everyone knows that they
make for the best soldiers in medieval
warfare.
What can I say? It's a Disney movie. Of
course, they had to have a girl boss at
the center of everything. Anyway, Ursa
and her mates accomplish their mission
with great vengeance and furious anger.
But it turns out that the real battle's
just beginning when a big burly predator
decides to have a go at them. What
follows is a brutal hand-to-hand fight
to the death with Ursa having to use
both strength and cunning to outsmart
and outfight her enemy. The second story
is set in feudal Japan where a ronin
named Koshi is out to kill his estranged
brother after a childhood confrontation
that drove them apart years earlier.
Again, there's a decent bit of action
and killing and some neat character
moments between them. But just as the
two men are facing off with each other,
they're interrupted by a predator who
decides this is his dojo now. But
they're not about to put up with that
[ __ ] And so the two brothers have to
put aside their differences and fight
together to take it down. The third
story is set during World War II where a
young pilot named Torres discovers that
a predator ship is attacking the other
aircraft in his squadron. Hm. A super
advanced spaceship armed with particle
beam weapons versus World War II fighter
planes. Guess who comes out on top
there? Anyway, Torres has to use his
unconventional flying skills and some
very quick thinking to take out the
enemy before it kills any more of his
men. And if that was where the movie
ended, I would have been fairly happy
with it. Yeah, the Torres story was kind
of goofy and the action scenes were so
far outside the bounds of reality that
it makes the Fast and the Furious
franchise look like the pinnacle of
gritty realism, but it followed through
on its premise to explore different
stories throughout human culture and
history and delivered a trilogy of neat
little self-contained vignettes that
don't outstay their welcome.
Unfortunately, it doesn't end there.
What we find out is that all three

where the winner has to take on the king

That doesn't seem fair. What the [ __ ] do

with no finale to tie the stories
together or for it to end on some kind
of cliffhanger where the survivors wake

so much quippy, self-aware humor and
implausible, gravitydefying fight scenes
that it feels more like a Marvel movie
than a Predator story. The plot armor
here is also off the charts. I lost
track of the number of times a Predator
picks a main character up by the neck
and just throws them across the room
when it could easily kill them
instantly. Or characters surviving falls
and impacts that should have absolutely
shattered every bone in their body. Or
predators somehow forgetting how to
fight the moment a tiny human leaps on
their back and starts hitting them.
Stuff like this is especially jarring
and disappointing because the overall
tone of the film is actually pretty
serious, especially the first two
stories that have strong character work
and genuinely compelling dramatic
moments. Yeah, you kind of have to look
past the obligatory girl boss elements

still not convinced is something I

of Killers is a frustrating kind of
movie where the idea is stronger than
the reality. An inventive concept ruined
by sloppy execution and a cheesy finale
that tries way too hard to set up the
next product for consumption. And I
guess that's the real problem here. Now
that there's a flicker of life in the
